whoToFollow.reason: Provides technical insights and ecosystem updates.
Shares real-world use cases and protocol fee data.

content.signal: Solana’s sixth anniversary highlights rapid ecosystem growth, major protocol upgrades, and new security considerations as usage and integrations surge.
content.story:
Solana just marked its sixth birthday, and the network is showing signs of both maturity and rapid expansion. The past week saw a wave of ecosystem milestones, including real-world payments, new protocol upgrades, and record-breaking usage stats. But as Solana cements its place in the crypto landscape, the pace of change brings new risks and open questions for users and builders alike.

### What's Happening
Solana’s network activity is hitting new highs, with over 518 billion transactions processed and $2.6 trillion in decentralized exchange volume to date. Stablecoin usage is up, and real-world use cases—like instant USDC-to-cash withdrawals in Thailand—are moving from theory to practice. The ecosystem’s top protocols are generating tens of millions in fees, and major integrations with financial heavyweights like Mastercard and Nasdaq are underway.

A major technical upgrade is also on the horizon. The upcoming P-token (SIMD-266) update, set to replace the current SPL Token program, promises to make on-chain operations cheaper and faster by reducing compute requirements. This is expected to lower transaction fees even further, reinforcing Solana’s reputation for speed and cost efficiency.

### Why It Matters
Solana’s ability to process high transaction volumes at low cost is attracting both users and developers. The network’s growth isn’t just speculative—real products and payments are being used globally. The success of protocols like Jupiter, Pump.fun, and Helium (now boasting millions of users and over 127,000 deployed hotspots) signals that Solana is supporting genuine economic activity, not just hype cycles.

The network’s integration with traditional finance—tokenized equities, insurance settlements, and stablecoin payments—suggests a strategic push to bridge crypto with mainstream financial infrastructure. If successful, this could position Solana as a core settlement layer for both Web3 and legacy systems. The approval and upcoming deployment of the P-token upgrade also shows that the ecosystem can adapt quickly to technical challenges and user needs.

### The Risk or Debate
Rapid growth and protocol changes come with real risks. As Solana’s transaction volume and user base expand, so does its attack surface. New integrations with financial institutions and the introduction of major upgrades like P-token increase the complexity of the network, potentially exposing new vulnerabilities. The transition to a new token program, while promising lower fees, will require careful monitoring for bugs or security lapses that could impact funds or network stability.

There’s also debate about whether Solana can maintain its low fees and high throughput as adoption scales further. The network’s history of outages and congestion is fresh in the minds of many users, and each new milestone raises the stakes for reliability and security.

### What To Watch
The rollout of the P-token upgrade in April will be a key test for Solana’s ability to deliver on its promises of efficiency and cost savings without sacrificing security. Watch for how quickly major protocols and wallets migrate to the new standard, and whether any issues emerge during the transition.

Continued growth in real-world usage—especially payments, stablecoins, and tokenized assets—will signal whether Solana can move beyond crypto-native circles. At the same time, keep an eye on how the network handles increased load and whether new vulnerabilities are discovered as complexity grows. The next quarter will be critical for proving Solana’s resilience as both a technical platform and a financial backbone.
takeaways: Solana is experiencing record usage, real-world adoption, and major protocol upgrades as it turns six.
The upcoming P-token upgrade aims to lower transaction fees and improve network efficiency.
Rapid growth and technical changes introduce new security and reliability risks that require close monitoring.

whoToFollow.reason: Provides hands-on comparisons and user insights into Solana neobanks.
Key builder in the Solana neobank space, setting standards for transparency.

content.signal: Solana-based crypto neobanks are aggressively competing on low fees, transparency, and user rewards to capture global users.
content.story:
Solana-native neobanks are ramping up competition, each aiming to deliver the most compelling blend of low fees, transparent pricing, and real-world utility. Recent user comparisons highlight how platforms like Jupiter Global, KAST, and Avici are differentiating themselves in the race to become the go-to crypto banking solution.

### What's Happening

Users are actively comparing Solana-powered neobanks on core features: top-up fees, transaction costs, cashback, and geographic reach. Jupiter Global stands out for its zero top-up fees and clear, upfront disclosure of all charges. KAST offers similar zero top-up fees, but with slightly higher non-USD transaction fees. Both platforms leverage Visa rails, meaning users may still face standard Visa FX fees, but the neobanks themselves are minimizing or eliminating their own charges.

Cashback and rewards are also becoming a key battleground. Jupiter Global advertises up to 10% USDC cashback and QR payment support, while KAST offers cashback, airdrops, and even a physical card. Real-world use cases are emerging, such as travelers withdrawing local cash from ATMs using USDC on Solana, demonstrating practical adoption beyond speculation.

### Why It Matters

This competition signals a maturing Solana ecosystem, where user experience and cost transparency are now central to product differentiation. By stripping away hidden fees and surfacing all costs, these neobanks are targeting crypto-native users frustrated with legacy banking friction. The focus on rewards and international usability also positions Solana neobanks as viable alternatives for global users, especially those underserved by traditional banks.

For the broader ecosystem, this trend could drive more stablecoin usage and on-chain activity, as users find it easier and cheaper to move between fiat and crypto. The push for transparency may also set new standards for how crypto financial products disclose fees and handle user funds.

### The Risk or Debate

Despite the advantages, users remain exposed to standard Visa fees and FX charges, which are outside the neobanks' control. There is also a risk that aggressive cashback or airdrop incentives may not be sustainable long-term, especially if user acquisition costs outpace revenue. Regulatory uncertainty around crypto banking, stablecoins, and cross-border payments could also impact service continuity or expansion into new markets.

Transparency claims will be tested as these platforms scale. Any hidden costs or sudden changes in fee structures could erode user trust quickly. Additionally, the reliance on third-party payment rails like Visa introduces dependencies that may limit how much control neobanks have over the total user experience.

### What To Watch

Watch for further product differentiation, especially around rewards, geographic expansion, and integration with local payment systems. User feedback on transparency and actual fee experiences will be critical in shaping reputations. Regulatory developments, particularly in the US and major international markets, could accelerate or constrain growth. Finally, expect other Solana projects to enter the neobank race, raising the bar for user experience and cost competitiveness.
takeaways: Solana neobanks are competing on low fees, transparency, and global usability.
Jupiter Global and KAST both offer zero top-up fees, but Visa FX fees still apply.
Cashback, airdrops, and real-world payment options are driving user adoption.

whoToFollow.reason: Shares detailed insights and results from building and deploying DLMM agents on Meteora.
Tracks top-performing pools and provides verified data on fee generation.

content.signal: Meteora’s liquidity pools are generating high fees, with AI-driven agent strategies delivering strong results for LPs.
content.story:
Meteora’s liquidity pools are seeing a surge in fee generation, with recent stats showing $41 per second, $2,491 per minute, and $149,473 per hour in fees. Over 90% of these fees are being distributed directly to liquidity providers. This growth is being fueled by advanced agent strategies, including AI-powered approaches that adapt and optimize liquidity positions in real time.

What's Happening

Builders and traders are deploying agent-driven strategies on Meteora, particularly through Dynamic Liquidity Market Making (DLMM). These agents are learning from top-performing LPs, experimenting with different strategies, and adjusting positions based on live data. Examples include classic bid-ask setups, evolving to spot strategies, and adjusting bin ranges to optimize for fee capture and impermanent loss management. Some agents are trained on historical data from successful LPs, allowing them to recognize patterns and act more autonomously.

Top-performing pools over the last 24 hours include pairs like SOS-SOL, Rosie-SOL, MEMECARD-SOL, and TRUMP-USDC, with LPs reporting consistent profits and high fee yields. Traders are sharing results from multi-day positions, noting that patience and well-chosen ranges can yield double-digit percentage returns, even after periods of drawdown.

Why It Matters

The rapid adoption of agent-driven strategies on Meteora signals a shift in how liquidity is managed on Solana. By leveraging AI and data-driven frameworks, LPs are able to automate complex decision-making and respond to market changes faster than manual strategies allow. This increases capital efficiency and can attract more liquidity to the platform.

High fee generation and direct distribution to LPs make Meteora an attractive venue for both experienced and newer participants. The ability for agents to learn from top LPs and adapt strategies in real time could set a new standard for liquidity management across the Solana ecosystem. This also demonstrates the growing sophistication of DeFi tooling, where automation and AI are becoming core components of trading and liquidity provision.

The Risk or Debate

While agent-driven strategies are delivering strong results, there are risks. Over-optimization or reliance on historical data can expose LPs to changing market conditions that the agent may not anticipate. Impermanent loss remains a factor, and not all strategies will perform equally across different tokens or market environments. There is also a learning curve for users to deploy and monitor these agents effectively.

What To Watch

The next phase to monitor is whether agent-driven LP strategies continue to outperform manual approaches as market conditions evolve. Watch for further innovation in agent frameworks, new pools attracting significant liquidity, and any shifts in fee distribution or pool performance. Community discussions and shared data on agent results will likely shape best practices and highlight emerging risks or opportunities.
takeaways: Meteora pools are generating over $149,000 in fees per hour, with most fees going to LPs.
AI-driven agent strategies are enabling LPs to adapt quickly and optimize returns.
Top pools and agent frameworks are setting new standards for automated liquidity management on Solana.
